author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Could this be made with Greek yogurt instead of mayo?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: To all the cooks who want to substitute or eliminate the anchovies: DO NOT make this dip. The anchovies in this recipe don’t taste fishy. They are the secret Unami bomb that makes this dip so incredibly tangy and delicious.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: This is the most delicious dip and definitely a keeper.  Not being a mayo fan, I used a 1/4 cup of mayo and subbed a large ripe avocado instead.  It's a healthier alternative and the results are very tasty, umami goodness.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: For heaven's sake, people, just use good mayonnaise. It's a dip- if there is no fat, there will be no taste. It's a party, or you wouldn't be making hors d'oeuvres. Make it taste good and don't eat too much!
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Not an anchovy fan, have you ever made this with something else?  Vegan or meat option perhaps?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Awesome dip. Made it exactly as written. Turned out great.  It's definitely on my favorites rotation dip recipes. What a recipe. Thanks. 
Understand why someone would try and bribe to get this recipe.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I have had a similar recipe to this that a dear friend gave me 35 yrs. ago.  I make it with a brick of cream cheese, 1 C. Mayonnaise, Anchovy paste, a clove of garlic & green onions. Fabulous. And nobody would ever guess that there are Anchovies in it.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: For those who love the anchovy umami effect but are fresh out of anchovies: fish sauce is mostly anchovy, and works in a pinch.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: In dips and dressings, the anchovy is usually not identifiable as such. Even the most staunch fish-hater would not be able to taste it. If you don't tell them it's there, they won't notice anything other than the delicious umami flavor.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Make it with mayonnaise and just eat three tablespoons of it. Then decide.  Mayo and yog don't have same characteristics. 

and to "Not an anchovy fan" - make this anyway and taste it before you say you don't like it.   Most anchovy recipes taste completely DELICIOUS in a seemingly undefinable way.... I never tell anyone there's anchovy in my vitello tonnato recipe - I don't have time to because they eat it so fast, just after saying they don't like anchovies.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Fond memories of The Store in East Hampton early 70s. I used to work in East Hampton in the summers as a nanny and cooked for the family. But I also lived on Long Island so my parents would go there occasionally when they were in the Hamptons. I remember Bert Greene's Ziti pasta salad. Was soooo good.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: For Stephanie - if you want a substitute for anchovies I once substituted miso paste for a couple of vegetarian friends, and it was really good!  (Personally I adore anchovies, but I like to keep friends happy, too!)

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Don’t be afraid of adding anchovies, even when you don’t like them. In a sauce like this they enhance the flavor and won’t turn it in to “anchovy” sauce. I’d use 1/3 mayo and 2/3 whole milk yogurt. Can’t wait to try it...today.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Even better than yogurt is the dip made ground raw cashews. You need a really good blender, Vitamix is the best. Grind 1/2 cup of cashews into a sandlike consistency. Add all the other ingredients and maybe 3/4 cup of water. Blend a little  and let sit 15 minutes. Then blend until smooth. Add water if it’s too thick.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Definitely some garlic, pounded with salt to a paste to dissolve into the background! Maybe twice the capers, a little basil... and home-made mayo with olive oil to take it up a step if there's enough time. Then devil some eggs with a little of it to go with the retro-tray of veggies. Delicious.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Answer re:  Yogurt sub for Mayo
I have subbed Greek yogurt to Mayo 50% - 50% w/ several recipes/dressings & it generally works well. You may have to adjust to your taste.
Enjoy!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I was freaked out at how much anchovy this called for, but NYT never lets me down, so I went for it. This vibrant dip was a huge hit. No one knew it was anchovy until I told them (and then it clicked). I tossed in the remainder of some plain yogurt I had that I didn't want to go to waste, and I think next time I'll do half and half with yogurt/mayo. I like mayonnaise, but it's a strong flavor here. I made it the day before and it held up great - didn't break, stayed a nice light green color.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Because I didn't have shallot or chives on hand I used one medium sized scallion and substituted vegenaise for mayo.  I also used flat leaf parsley and upped it to a scant cup.  Delicious!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: In this sort of context, anchovies are like fish sauce -- they disappear as an identifiable ingredient but add impressive depth of flavor. While I would never mislead someone about what was in something I served, I think you might try the recipe as written and see if anchovy-haters will agree that it's good.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I made this the other day and it's wonderful. The anchovies (I used best quality Cantabrian ones from Spain) and the anchovy oil make this dip sing. I made my own quick mayo from scratch but next time I'll try good store bought for comparison. If anyone wants to see how I presented the dish check out my insta link: https://www.instagram.com/p/B14ei6hHQIN/?utm_source=ig_web_button_native_share

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: This sounds amazing, I love all the ingredients. Can't wait to hit my local farmers market now!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: 1 container 5% Chobani Greek yogurt + 1 small avocado subbed for mayo. Delicious!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I'm allergic to seafood, including anchovies, to which I'm especially sensitive. Can you recommend a substitute, or do those of us with such allergies just have to skip this one? (Also - note to other cooks, please warn guests if you use anchovies, anchovy paste, or even Worcestershire sauce to flavor foods where this is not expected. I see the ingredient creeping into more and more recipes as a flavor enhancer, but many are allergic to seafood, including anchovies.) Thanks!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: If you're looking for a little balm for the soul, Bert Greene's cookbooks are just that. His intros to each recipe are kind and funny vignettes that capture the best of people and the food they create. He was a lovely man.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Do not use a regular size Vitamix jar because there is not enough substance to “Almost liquefy”. I had to switch to a mini food chopper in order to get the anchovy mix to the correct consistency.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to technique or equipment

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I have been making this dressing since the book was published and I would opt for scallions over chives every single time!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Very similar to the Florentine Dip, which inspired me to try this dip with a greek yogurt (1 cup) and sour cream (1/2 cup) base. It was delicious! Definitely more my style than a mayonnaise dip. 

Did not use food processor (didn't feel like washing it) - just hand mixed and it was fine. I was afraid the 2 tablespoons of red wine vinegar would loosen up the dip too much but it was fine and tasted better with the 2nd tablespoon.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
I use the Vegan Anchovy Substitute from America's Test Kitchen Plant Based Cookbook.  

Use 1 tsp. to replace either 1 anchovy fillet or 1/2 tsp. anchovy paste.

Blend:
1/4 cup white miso
1 tsp. nori powder

If you can't find nori powder you can grind 1 sheet of crumbled toasted nori in a spice grinder to fine powder. Do not use snack-size sheets.
I put a small piece of Saran wrap over the top of my electric spice grinder before grinding to keep nori from flying out of the grinding container.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made with 0% greek yogurt (what we had) and added olive oil to compensate. In addition to the parsley, added in the languishing extra herbs - handful or so of cilantro and dill. Very tasty if not quite what's described here. Definitely improves the longer it's chilled - better after 24hrs (plus a squeezed lemon wedge to perk it up).

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
When I'm making this for just me I use overripe avocado, lowfat yogurt, and just a dollop of Duke's mayo instead of all mayo. Equally delicious imo and much lower guilt. I also like to add a serrano or 2 to feed my spice addiction. Extra green! I thin it a bit to use as salad dressing sometimes, because why not? 

Just FYI, I disagree that you can't taste the anchovy when made exactly as instructed. Not a problem for me at all, but for anchovy haters I wouldn't use more than half the tin.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: 2/3 guests i had literally spat out and one threw up at the intense anchovy taste. Whoever said you can't taste it has to be lying. It might be the worst recipe I made in teh NYT.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Very good. I forgot to rinse the capers and I regretted it as it was a bit salty - and I love salty. Next time I will rinse the capers for sure.  Still, really good.  Enjoyed with a platter of veggies and crackers.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I hadn’t made this in a while and forgot how crazy good it is—simply the perfect veggie dip and a delicious departure from tired old ranch. If you’ve got your tinsel in a tangle over the anchovies, make something else. The anchovies are the linchpin of the whole thing.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: We subbed Caprino (goat cheese) and sour cream for mayo. We added a shot of Colatura di Alici (an Italian version of Thai fish sauce) and a sprinkle of Tajin. This is a major hit at aperitivo hour.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I added a clove of garlic and loved the result.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Delicious!  Such nice layers of flavor.  Used a cup of sour cream and half a cup of mayo.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: This dip needs more than 1 hour in the fridge to cool down and mellow out. I made it for a party and it spent the minimum 1 hour in the fridge before serving. The flavor was fairly strong and it was not a favorite dip for most of the guests. I had only put out ~1/2 cup but even that was barely touched. I left what wasn't used in the fridge until the next day and tried again with some carrots - totally different and much more enjoyable flavor profile!
readable_ai_guess: Thought the cooking times were wrong

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
I have had a similar recipe to this that a dear friend gave me 35 yrs. ago. I make it with a brick of cream cheese, 1 C. Mayonnaise, Anchovy paste, a clove of garlic & green onions. Fabulous. And nobody would ever guess that there are Anchovies in it.
You could try miso for that shot of umami. I'd suggest about 2-3 TBS. I'll give it a go since my husband, although an omnivore, is not a fan of anchovies. You'd probably have to add about 2 tsp of olive oil to balance it out.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Oh my goodness, this is Soooooo good.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: This is delicious and well worth the effort when you are having folks over for happy hour.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made as directed but subbing Greek yoghurt for half the Mayo.  Wow!  Best dip I have ever had.  Can’t wait to use as a salad dressing, too.  I think I may try next time with 2/3rds yoghurt and 1/3rd Mayo to lighten it up a bit…. Really good and so easy

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Not good.  Don’t make this dip.  We threw most of it out after our guests avoided it at a cocktail party.  And we followed the recipe exactly except used red onion instead of chives.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I firmly believe that the use of higher quality anchovies is key here.  There is a huge difference in taste.  One of the best brands is "Anchovies de L'Escala in olive oil, from Spain.  They’re hand-packed in jars, with excellent, meaty, filets,  not at all fishy tasting.   Overall, excellent recipe.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I used the whole anchovy instead of the fillet. I think that was a mistake. Couldn’t find the 2 oz can at 2 different stores. It’s still good but intensely flavored. Live and learn

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Even though I used more than the stated amount of parsley, this dip tasted so strongly of anchovy that I had to throw it away. I have nothing against anchovies and use them in other dishes. Perhaps there is simply too much variation between anchovy brands for the results to be consistent.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made exactly as instructed and it turned out delicious. Would also make a great dip for chilled seafood or steamed artichoke.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I made this with a mix of Forager Cashewgurt (yeah, a vegan yogurt...) and mayo. It turned out well - I used to eat Greek yogurt and I do know the tang of it is very unappealing in a dip for some people. This vegan stuff has no strange or unappealing aftertaste and couldn’t be detected when mixed in.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: The first time I made this I stuck with the recipe and although I like mayonnaise there was just too much of it to suit my taste. The second time I used 1 c mayonnaise and 1/2 c full fat goat yogurt and it’s perfect.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Everyone who says you can’t taste the anchovies … I made this over the weekend, and the taste of them was overwhelming to the point that none of my guests would even touch the stuff. A waste of time and money.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Two best ingredients...mayo and anchovies what’s not to love?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made with scallions, garlic, fancy anchovies not-in-can, half siggis whole fat and half lite 365 mayo. Blanched (do this!) thin orange and purple carrots and parsnips from farmers. Brown cherry tomatoes and scallions. At first did not like it, but changed our minds as we kept eating it. Next time maybe avocado instead of siggis? And more kinds of vegs, like broccoli and sno/snap peas. It makes A LOT of dip.  We had to stop eating and there's at least 3/4 of it left.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Kinda feel like the mayo overpowers this here. Anyone else?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
I like cutting the mayonnaise (I use homemade) with Greek yogurt--1/3--1.2 cup
or however much tastes good on the day I make it.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I didn’t like this. It was super heavy since it’s basically all mayo. The anchovies added a nice flavor but I couldn’t eat much of it and neither could anyone I served it to at a dinner party.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made this as directed.  I did find it very fishy and with a heavy mayo taste. I ended up adding a ripe avocado as recommended to cut the mayo flavor. If I make it again,I will reduce mayo, keep the avocado,  and maybe half the amount of anchovy and omit its oil.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: In contrast to others, I really REALLY tasted the anchovies and it was overwhelming. I love the idea of this dip and it came together beautifully but the anchovy flavor was not to my taste. I added lemon in an attempt to make this more palatable for me but sadly, it didn’t help. I did make it with an avocado and mayo so for anyone else wondering if this is a decent sub, it is. Creamy, thick but taste was not for me.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Very OK to make with yogurt, or combo of mayo/yogurt.

I have had great success making not just dips but sauces for fish and meat using unhomogenized yogurt, which must be stirred briefly before using and produces a more liquid-y mix which is great for sauces.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: why are the capers rinsed?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
I used lactose-free yogurt to sub for half the mayo, upped the parsley by a few sprigs, and made one critical mistake — I started off in my blender, not my wee food processor. Use the food processor! At least for blending the herbs and anchovy, etc. 

It’s also perfectly delicious without black pepper.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made it yesterday for an appy board. It was a hit! So delicious!
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: What a delightful surprise to read Sam Sifton's article about Bert Greene's  "The Store Cookbook" today. The added bonus is all the ingredients are in my frig already!  The book  has its honored place among many in my cookbook library, well worn from years of use at home and in a catering business from long ago.    I have torn out the article from today's magazine to tuck away inside the cookbook's front cover for posterity.    Some days, looking back is really fun.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made mine with half Greek yoghurt and more basil than chives since that is what I had. All the anchovy-haters loved it. Am going to try it with deviled eggs, great suggestion!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: easy substitution for anchovies is Worcestershire sauce

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: The first time I made this dip I had less than the called-for 2 oz. can of anchovies on hand so I used about half that amount and the dip was a hit. I made it again yesterday with the full amount and found it too salty and the anchovy flavor a bit overpowering- and I love anchovies. Next time I’ll go back to using only 4 anchovies.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
A trip down culinary memory lane—obviously, those small glass jars of Spanish and/or Italian anchovies weren’t available in the 1970s (much easier to deal with than the flat cans).
Also, the idea of rinsing the capers (I always do) is to remove any excess brininess (or salt, if using salt-packed ones).

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I used 1/2 Greek yogurt and 1/2 mayo (because I didn’t have enough mayo on hand). I also increased the vinegar by a tablespoon and also an extra 1/4 cup of parsley.  My main note, however, is that this is delicious straight out of the blender but it tastes even better after sitting in the fridge for a few hours.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Wonderful dip. I made just as directed using chives rather than shallots. Everything fit in my 3-cup Mini-Prep food processor; so clean up was a snap.  Planning to make another batch soon!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Don’t fear the anchovies. You won’t know that they are even there except for that amazing punch of some salty deliciousness. Jump all in on this one! You will be glad you did!
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
I made this dip and even though I don’t love anchovies and really don’t like capers, it was phenomenal!  I was too conservative with the parsley, which was a mistake (added some later).  It is a magical recipe that proves that the sum is great than all its parts.

Question:  Can anyone tell me how long I can keep this in the refrigerator???

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I made this and used it as a sauce for crab cakes. Yum!

author: Sam Sifton

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made almost as written, using shallots and subbing 1/2 c whole milk yogurt for an equal part mayo. Good.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Yummm. Do not leave the anchovies out. Umami bomb. Would love to find a way to make this slightly less mayo heavy (purely a taste preference) but loved it overall.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I make a very similar dip with silken tofu instead of the mayo, not because we're vegan (anchovies are delicious) but because we don't like the texture of mayo and because dip + veg (+ crackers if you're inclined) then equals a healthy, reasonably protein-balanced meal.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Delicious! I had trouble getting thins liquified in step 1...but once I added 1/2 mayo and 1/2 greek yogurt, it all blended nicely. Refrigeration is an important step as it made the consistency just right. Served with homemade herb pita chips.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Delicious! Quick and easy to put together with whatever green herbs you have. I had some parsley (not enough), a lot of basil, chives, and some tarragon in the garden. I included a chopped clove of garlic and doubled the anchovies. I started by making the mayonnaise, scraped it out of the food processor but didn't rinse it out, put in the rest of the ingredients, and added the mayo back in. I'll try it with the recommended amount of parsley next time -- even if I have to buy it.

Avocado and yogurt?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: We used Caprino (goat cheese) and sour cream in lieu of Mayo. We added a shot of Colatura di Alici (the Italian equivalent of Thai fish sauce), a bit of Tajin, and roasted garlic. This was a smash hit for aperitivo hour.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Made for a big family gathering and received only thumbs down (I promise, no toxic family dynamics reflected in their reviews). I prepared exactly as written and used fresh parsley and chives from my garden. The typical reaction:

A smile - “Oh, this looks amazing!” Grab a carrot or pepper, dip in, take a bite - still smiling. Smile disappears and nose wrinkles. “Why does this taste like fish?”

So for us, more than umami.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I have made my own mayo for 3 years now. It’s a 20 minute kitchen event about once every three weeks. Use quality oil and vinegars, lemon juice and Dijon. Use a blender or a food processor—they both work. You will never buy the shelf stuff again and you will also never again worry about substitutions for that tasteless gross store bought stuff.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: For the vegans in the chat: I did use miso as suggested, and some extra capers, along with vegan mayo.  I have no idea if it tastes as intended, but what I did make is delicious!  Vegan Worcestershire might be a good substitute for the anchovies too.  Really good starting point for the dietarily restricted, and I’m sure straight-up delicious for everyone else!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: This dip was fabulous with fresh veggies, baked pita triangles and even sturdy potato chips. I used 3 parts mayo and 2 parts greek yogurt. Sour cream and mayo would also work. Used 2 tbsp each of chives and shallots to amp up the flavor. Very quick prep time. It would make a great sandwich or burger condiment too.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: to Sydney: personally, I almost always find the suggested additions, subtractions, modifications to be at minimum interesting and informative, and generally implement them.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I used about half the anchovies suggested and still found it much too fishy for my taste. The dip tastes strongly of anchovy, as opposed to a caesar dressing where they blend in. I would suggest starting with just a few and tasting as you go. I used homemade mayonnaise for this and subbed roughly half for greek yogurt. The dip was otherwise excellent. I think you may find it more watery with half greek yogurt instead of mayonnaise only, which is more gelatinous.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Excellent dip/accompaniment for vegetables. The anchovies add a punch of flavor that can’t be skipped. I know some people are “anti anchovy” buy they are critical to this recipe. I thought the dip was better after sitting for twenty four hours. Also, I used the primal kitchen mayo (with avocado oil) because I realized I was out of Duke’s...halfway through making this dip. I do not recommend deviating from standard mayo because the lack of any sugar in the mayo was problematic.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: To make this slightly lighter, I used equal parts mayonnaise and natural yoghurt. It was delicious served with grilled sea bass, crushed roasted new potatoes, and green vegetables.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I made it just as the recipe is and it was fantastic. Super tasty. It doesn't taste "fishy" at all but more savory and salty like a Cesar dressing. I would like to sub Greek yogurt just to make it a healthy weekday snack option. I can imagine using this as a topping to salmon or on as a spread on a breakfast sandwich. Yum!
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made as directed, it's delicious. All taste are subjective, but for me it wasn't too fishy. A little goes a long way. Does great after overnight (might even be better), though it loses a bit of its bite. I'll be putting this one the list! Hellmans mayo for me. Delicious on anything, chips, crudité, toast, and I can't wait to try it on fish. If you're a fan of homemade Ceasar this hits many of the same notes with the anchovy bit more forward and the red wine vinegar playing the part of the lemon.
readable_ai_guess: Liked recipe and prepared as written

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: We call this Beard dip in our household after the famous James whose dip was almost identical (sans anchovies and vinegar).  Our favorite for decades and is delicious on every single thing you can think to put it on.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I didn't have anchovies on hand the first time I made it, so subbed with anchovy paste & it was a big hit. Have also made it with the anchovies, but didn't go over as well. Maybe it has to do with the brand/type or anchovy? Any suggestions for good quality anchovies?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made this exactly as written noting that the anchovy fans said don’t dis the umami main ingredient. “It won’t taste fishy” they said. Well, the fat intensified the anchovies.  And had to throw it out. Maybe an inch or two of anchovy paste or I am thinking about using some brewers yeast flakes because I am drawn to the creamy herbyness of the rest of this dip.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I echo others' preferences for a more yogurt/sour cream base to dips. I love mayo (especially Kewpie!) but my 1 cup greek yogurt and 1/2 cup Kewpie was still slightly too mayo-forward for me. I don't think I'll have any trouble gobbling this up tonight, but I think with this particular ratio it will be even better as a dip for french fries or as a sauce for a burger.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Delicious. Do not omit anchovies! Not fishy but give it depth. At end the color wasn’t as green as above so I added a handful of spinach. Really delicious. Could be tasty on baked salmon. Or in a baked potato.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: This is delicious. I made as directed, used white anchovies because that’s what I had. All the comments about this not being anchovy dominant are spot on. It’s all about the flavor. This will be part of my repertoire.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
Caution – label this as contains fish – It just doesn’t look like it would contain fish and it may not even smell that way since parsley tends to counter fishy odors.  For the safety and well-being of people with fish and seafood allergies The label is critical. Most would not
expect a green dip to be vegetable based and not ask before tasting.  A large attached label that cannot be missed – Could save a trip to the emergency room or even save a life.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I made this yesterday for a family barbecue, using 5 oz while milk Greek yogurt and 8 oz mayo. The dip is delicious!  Of note:  it was a bit runny yesterday but today it has thickened up to an excellent dipping consistency with no loss of color so I recommend making it a day in advance.   It makes a large amount (about cups plus).

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Whoever said this recipe is not fishy is crazy. I also made it with Greek yogurt, and avocado, and cream cheese which should've even cut down on the fishiness. It tasted like a green fish dip. But not in a good way. Waste of ingredients
